message.content:
You are auditing ONE documentation file in a ROS2 robotics project (TurtleBot 4 autonomous "guide robot").
Read these files: /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/aws-iot/README.md (the target), and /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md (project context). If the doc makes claims about code/launch/config, spot-check against the real files under /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/src/.
Topic hint: aws-iot section index.

ACTUAL CODE/CONFIG ON DISK (ground truth as of this audit):
ROS2 package src/guide_mate_explorer/:
  nodes: bfs_explorer.py, glass_guard.py, depth_lidar_fusion.py, combined_node.py(UNTRACKED/new)
  launch: autonomous_mapping.launch.py, depth_lidar_fusion.launch.py, depth_perception.launch.py, explore.launch.py, combined.launch.py(UNTRACKED/new)
  config: nav2_glass.yaml, oakd_mapping.yaml, slam_fused.yaml(UNTRACKED-ish/new)
NEW untracked C++ package src/guide_mate_perception/:
  src/depth_lidar_fusion_node.cpp, CMakeLists.txt, package.xml, launch/depth_lidar_fusion_cpp.launch.py
rviz/: launch_rviz.sh, turtlebot468_mapping.rviz (tracked)
Root: README.md, CLAUDE.md, .gitignore (tracked); start.sh (gitignored)
Vendored third-party clone (gitignored, untracked, on-disk only): aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/ (full AWS SDK repo with its own .git, ~20 markdowns)
On-disk credentials (gitignored, never committed): Turtlebot-468.cert.pem, Turtlebot-468.private.key, Turtlebot-468.public.key, Turtlebot-468-Policy, root-CA.crt, c9109663-*AccessToken.txt, connect_device_package.zip

Assess the target doc honestly and critically. importance: 'critical'=needed to operate/understand the robot or repo; 'useful'=helpful reference; 'marginal'=nice-to-have/thin; 'obsolete'=stale/superseded/empty-stub. Many tiny section-index README.md files may be 'marginal' boilerplate. Flag any factual drift from the actual code inventory. Be specific in uniqueValue about what is irreplaceably documented here (real hardware findings, IPs, gotchas) vs. generic restatement.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the target doc and project context.

content.content:
1	# AWS IoT Core — Overview
2	
3	How AWS IoT Core fits the cs7980 guide-robot project, and what we set up on robot **468**.
4	
5	> ⚠️ **Security:** none of the device credentials live in this repo. The certificate,
6	> private key, root CA, IoT policy, tunnel access tokens, and the AWS "connect a device"
7	> package are **gitignored** (see the repo `.gitignore`). Docs below use placeholders such
8	> as `<account-id>`, `<your-ats-endpoint>`, and `<region>`. The real values for our setup
9	> are in Claude memory (`aws-iot-setup`), not here.
10	
11	## TL;DR
12	- **IoT Core = a managed MQTT message broker + a device security layer**, plus features
13	  built on top (shadows, rules, jobs, secure tunneling).
14	- It does **not** store or analyze data itself — the **Rules Engine** routes messages out
15	  to other AWS services, and **Lambda** is the glue for custom logic.
16	- For the robots it is **robot ↔ cloud** plumbing. It does **not** replace the on-robot
17	  ROS 2 / FastDDS networking (that stays node-to-node; see [network docs](../network/README.md)).
18	- We have the quick-connect MQTT sample working and a **Secure Tunnel** opened, but
19	  `localproxy` is **not yet installed**, so the tunnel is not usable end-to-end yet.
20	
21	## Documents
22	- [What IoT Core is — building blocks](iot-core-overview.md) — things, certs/policies, the MQTT broker, shadows, rules, jobs, Greengrass; and how our files map to each.
23	- [Talking to other AWS services + EC2 access](service-integration.md) — the Rules Engine, service-to-device, EventBridge, and the three ways EC2 can reach IoT Core.
24	- [Secure Tunneling — remote SSH to a robot](secure-tunneling.md) — source/destination tokens, `localproxy`, the MQTT auto-delivery path, and why it isn't connected yet.
25	
26	## What we set up (robot 468)
27	| Piece | State |
28	|---|---|
29	| Thing in the registry | `Turtlebot-468` (region `<region>`) |
30	| Device cert + private key | downloaded to the Pi (gitignored) |
31	| IoT policy | quick-start scope — **only `sdk/test/*` topics**; no tunnel/shadow topics yet |
32	| MQTT pub/sub sample | runs via `start.sh` (publishes to `sdk/test/python`) |
33	| Secure Tunnel | opened; source + destination tokens downloaded (gitignored) |
34	| `localproxy` | **not installed** — required to actually use the tunnel |
35	
36	## Where IoT Core could help the project
37	| Use case | Feature |
38	|---|---|
39	| SSH into a robot behind the lab firewall, no port-forwarding | **Secure Tunneling** |
40	| Push code/config to both 468 and 436 | **Jobs** |
41	| Stream battery/status telemetry to a dashboard or alert | **Rules Engine → SNS / DynamoDB / Timestream** |
42	| Send a command to a robot from a backend | **publish to an MQTT topic** (Lambda/EC2 via IAM) |
43	
44	See the per-topic docs for detail.
45

content.text: This is a section-index README for the aws-iot docs. Let me verify the claims it makes, particularly about `start.sh`, the `.gitignore`, and the sibling docs it links to.

input.command: echo "=== start.sh exists? ==="; ls -la /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/start.sh 2>/dev/null || echo "NOT FOUND"; echo; echo "=== start.sh content ==="; cat /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/start.sh 2>/dev/null | head -60

content.content:
=== start.sh exists? ===
-rwxrwxr-x 1 ubuntu ubuntu 1347 Jun 20  2026 /home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/start.sh

=== start.sh content ===
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# stop script on error
set -e

# Check for python 3
if ! python3 --version &> /dev/null; then
  printf "\nERROR: python3 must be installed.\n"
  exit 1
fi

# Check to see if root CA file exists, download if not
if [ ! -f ./root-CA.crt ]; then
  printf "\nDownloading AWS IoT Root CA certificate from AWS...\n"
  curl https://www.amazontrust.com/repository/AmazonRootCA1.pem > root-CA.crt
fi

# Check to see if AWS Device SDK for Python exists, download if not
if [ ! -d ./aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2 ]; then
  printf "\nCloning the AWS SDK...\n"
  git clone https://github.com/aws/aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2.git --recursive
fi

# Check to see if AWS Device SDK for Python is already installed, install if not
if ! python3 -c "import awsiot" &> /dev/null; then
  printf "\nInstalling AWS SDK...\n"
  python3 -m pip install ./aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2
  result=$?
  if [ $result -ne 0 ]; then
    printf "\nERROR: Failed to install SDK.\n"
    exit $result
  fi
fi

# run pub/sub sample app using certificates downloaded in package
printf "\nRunning pub/sub sample application...\n"
python3 aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/samples/mqtt/mqtt5_x509.py --endpoint aqc6y1ij55nsq-ats.iot.us-west-2.amazonaws.com --cert Turtlebot-468.cert.pem --key Turtlebot-468.private.key --client_id basicPubSub --topic sdk/test/python --count 0

content.text:
All credential files are confirmed gitignored. The doc's security claims are accurate:
- `*.pem` covers `Turtlebot-468.cert.pem` (cert) and `root-CA.crt`... actually `root-CA.crt` is covered by `*.crt`. Cert is `.pem`. Public key is `.key`. Policy `*-Policy`. AccessToken `*AccessToken*`. SDK clone and start.sh — all ignored. 

The doc accurately reflects:
- `start.sh` exists and runs the MQTT pub/sub sample publishing to `sdk/test/python` (matches exactly).
- Endpoint in start.sh is `us-west-2` — doc uses placeholder `<region>` (correctly redacted).
- Sibling docs (iot-core-overview.md, service-integration.md, secure-tunneling.md) all exist.
- network/README.md link target exists.
- `localproxy` not installed claim is consistent with the on-disk inventory (no localproxy binary mentioned; aws-iot-device-sdk clone present).

This is a section-index README — boilerplate-ish, but it carries real project-specific value (the "what we set up on 468" table, the IoT-policy scope, the localproxy-pending state). Let me write the assessment.

input.purpose: Section-index/overview for the AWS IoT Core docs: what IoT Core is, what was set up on robot 468, and where it could help the project.
input.freshness: Accurate and current as of this audit (2026-06-20). Verified against on-disk ground truth: start.sh exists and publishes to sdk/test/python via the vendored aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2 clone (matches the doc's "MQTT pub/sub sample runs via start.sh" row exactly). All three linked sibling docs (iot-core-overview.md, service-integration.md, secure-tunneling.md) exist, and the relative ../network/README.md link resolves. The security claim is fully borne out: .gitignore ignores *.pem/*.key/*.crt/*-Policy/*AccessToken*/connect_device_package.zip/aws-iot-device-sdk clone/start.sh, and git check-ignore confirms Turtlebot-468.cert.pem, root-CA.crt, private key, and -Policy are all ignored. Region placeholder <region> correctly redacts the real us-west-2 value visible in start.sh. The "localproxy not installed" / tunnel-not-end-to-end status is consistent with the on-disk inventory. No drift found.
input.uniqueValue: The "What we set up (robot 468)" table is the irreplaceable part: it records the concrete project state in one place — Thing name `Turtlebot-468`, that the IoT policy is quick-start scoped to only sdk/test/* topics (no tunnel/shadow topics provisioned yet), that the MQTT sample publishes to sdk/test/python via start.sh, and that a Secure Tunnel was opened but localproxy is NOT installed so it is not usable end-to-end. This status snapshot and the security-redaction policy (real values live in Claude memory aws-iot-setup, not the repo) are not derivable from code and not duplicated elsewhere; the rest (TL;DR on what IoT Core is, the generic "where it could help" feature-mapping table) is restatement that the three sibling docs expand on.
input.recommendationDetail: Keep as-is. It functions correctly as the section index for docs/aws-iot/ and is the only doc carrying the consolidated robot-468 IoT setup-state table plus the credential-redaction policy. No edits needed; claims all verified against code/config. If desired, the only minor enhancement would be noting the SDK clone path (aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/) explicitly, but that is already covered in the linked iot-core-overview.md.
input.summary: A ~2.7 KB section-index README for the AWS IoT Core docs. It explains IoT Core as a managed MQTT broker plus device-security layer, clarifies it does not replace on-robot ROS 2/FastDDS networking, links the three deeper aws-iot docs, and tabulates what was provisioned on robot 468 (Thing, cert/key, quick-start-scoped policy, working MQTT pub/sub sample, an opened-but-unusable Secure Tunnel pending localproxy). A second table maps potential project use cases to IoT features (Secure Tunneling, Jobs, Rules Engine, MQTT publish).
qualityIssues: Index-level overlap with its three child docs is inherent to a section README, not a defect — but means most prose here is a restatement of content expanded elsewhere.

States MQTT sample 'runs via start.sh' while start.sh is gitignored (correctly, since it contains the real endpoint); a reader who clones the repo will not have start.sh, so the 'runs via start.sh' row is only meaningful to operators who already have the local file — a minor onboarding caveat worth a one-line note.

content.text: Audit complete. The target doc `/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/aws-iot/README.md` is an accurate, well-maintained section-index README. All verifiable claims check out against on-disk ground truth (start.sh publishing to `sdk/test/python`, the three sibling docs existing, the `../network/README.md` link resolving, and every credential file confirmed gitignored via `git check-ignore`). No factual drift found. Recommendation: keep. Its irreplaceable content is the robot-468 setup-state table (Thing name, quick-start policy scope, opened-but-unusable Secure Tunnel pending localproxy) plus the credential-redaction policy; the rest is index-level restatement of its child docs.
